seasonal_wine.name: Winter pruning on the slate, January to February
seasonal_wine.description: Through the coldest months growers prune the bare slate terraces by hand, often roped onto slopes too steep for any machine. The dormant vineyards reveal the raw blue and grey rock that defines Mosel Riesling. Cellars are quiet, with the young vintage settling on its lees.
seasonal_wine.tip: Winter is the calmest time to book an unhurried estate tasting; many growers are home and happy to pour older library vintages by the fire.

seasonal_wine.name: Eiswein harvest window, December to January
seasonal_wine.recurrence_pattern: annual but vintage-dependent, December to January overnight frosts
seasonal_wine.description: When the temperature drops below minus seven, growers who left grapes hanging rush to pick frozen berries before dawn for Eiswein, pressing the concentrated juice while the water stays as ice. Warm winters mean some years yield no Eiswein at all, which only sharpens its prestige.
seasonal_wine.tip: Eiswein is a gamble on the weather; ask estates whether the current vintage produced any, as several recent warm winters delivered none.

seasonal_wine.name: Bud break on the terraces, April
seasonal_wine.description: As April warms the slate, the first green shoots break along the terraces and the steep slopes turn from grey to fresh leaf. Growers tie down canes and begin the long season of vineyard work that on the steepest sites is done entirely by hand.
seasonal_wine.tip: Spring light on the budding terraces makes for the best vineyard photography of the year before the canopy fills in.

seasonal_wine.name: Strausswirtschaft season opens, spring
seasonal_wine.description: Growers hang a wreath of straw or a bouquet over the door to signal their seasonal Strausswirtschaft is open, pouring estate wine with simple home cooking in a courtyard or cellar. These pop-up taverns are the most authentic way to drink Mosel Riesling at its source.
seasonal_wine.tip: Look for the straw bouquet over a grower's door; it means the family is pouring their own wine that evening, often cash only.

seasonal_wine.name: Mythos Mosel Riesling weekend, June
seasonal_wine.recurrence_pattern: annual, weekend after Whitsun, late May or June
seasonal_wine.description: The Moseljuenger young-grower association opens dozens of estates between Detzem and Wintrich for a weekend Riesling ride, with vintage tastings, special pours and cellar parties. It is the single best chance to taste the breadth of the Middle Mosel in one trip.
seasonal_wine.tip: Buy the wristband in advance and plan stations geographically; the riverside villages cluster tightly so you can walk or shuttle between several growers.

seasonal_wine.name: Canopy work and veraison, July to August
seasonal_wine.description: High summer is canopy management: trimming, leaf-pulling and green-harvesting on baking slate slopes. By late August the berries soften and colour at veraison. Riverside terraces are at their most lush, and the wine festivals begin rolling along the valley.
seasonal_wine.tip: Summer evenings on the Mosel promenades are made for a chilled trocken Riesling; most estate vinotheks pour by the glass on terraces over the river.

seasonal_wine.name: SaarRieslingSommer, late August
seasonal_wine.description: The Saar's top estates open stations across the valley for a late-August weekend of Riesling tasting, a more intimate sibling to the Middle Mosel events. The Saar's cooler, racier style of Riesling is the headline.
seasonal_wine.tip: The Saar makes Germany's most piercingly mineral Riesling; this weekend is the easiest way to compare the top Wiltingen and Saarburg growers side by side.

seasonal_wine.name: Riesling harvest, late September to November
seasonal_wine.recurrence_pattern: annual, late September to November with selective passes
seasonal_wine.description: The defining season. Pickers make multiple passes up the steep slate to sort grapes for everything from bone-dry GG to nobly sweet Auslese, choosing each bunch by ripeness and botrytis. The long, cool Mosel autumn is what lets Riesling hang into November.
seasonal_wine.tip: Harvest visits need to be arranged directly with a grower; it is the busiest time, but watching selective picking on a 60-degree slope is unforgettable.

seasonal_wine.name: Federweisser and Zwiebelkuchen, autumn
seasonal_wine.description: As the first juice ferments, the cloudy, fizzy, half-fermented Federweisser appears in taverns across the valley, traditionally drunk with warm Zwiebelkuchen onion tart. It is the taste of the Mosel autumn and the heart of the season's village festivals.
seasonal_wine.tip: Federweisser keeps fermenting in the bottle, so never seal it tight and drink it within a day or two; pair it with onion tart at any autumn village fest.

seasonal_wine.name: Weinfest der Mittelmosel, early September
seasonal_wine.recurrence_pattern: annual, five days around the first Sunday in September
seasonal_wine.description: Bernkastel-Kues throws one of the largest wine festivals on the whole river around the first Sunday in September, with a wine route through the medieval old town, the crowning of the Mosella, a parade and fireworks over the river.
seasonal_wine.tip: Beds in Bernkastel sell out months ahead for the festival weekend; base yourself in a neighbouring village and walk in along the river.

seasonal_wine.name: New dry-vintage and GG release, September
seasonal_wine.description: The VDP releases its top dry wines, the Grosse Gewaechse, from the previous vintage in early September, a fixed point on the German calendar. Mosel GG Rieslings from sites like Marienburg and Roettgen reach the market and trade tastings.
seasonal_wine.tip: Mosel GGs are made in tiny quantities; if you want a specific site, order from the estate or a specialist as soon as the September release lands.

seasonal_wine.name: Trier autumn fine-wine auction, September
seasonal_wine.description: The historic Trier wine auctions, run through the VDP's regional association, sell tiny lots of the rarest Mosel, Saar and Ruwer Rieslings, including aged Auslese and Eiswein. Prices set here signal the value of the region's most prized bottlings each year.
seasonal_wine.tip: Bidding is mostly through trade agents, but the pre-auction tastings are open to serious buyers and are a rare chance to taste museum-level old Mosel.
